---
title: "09 · Classical computer vision"
subtitle: "How it worked before deep learning — edges, contours, features, and flow"
---

::: {.callout-note title="New here? Read this first" collapse="true"}
No prior knowledge needed — but this report is easier if you've skimmed
**[Foundations](../../foundations.qmd)** (how a computer sees an image, what a model is, how we
measure success). Stuck on a word? See the **[Glossary](../../glossary.qmd)**. Stuck on a model
name? See **[Model families](../../models.qmd)**. Key terms are explained inline the first time they
appear.
:::

## Why this module exists

Modules 01–08 are all neural networks. This one deliberately goes back to the **pre-deep-learning**
toolkit — *classical CV*, meaning hand-designed algorithms (explicit math), with no training and no
neural networks. These come from OpenCV (a free, widely used library of ready-made image-processing
functions; see [Model families](../../models.qmd)) and are **fast, transparent, deterministic, and
need no training data or GPU**. *Deterministic* means the same input always gives the same output —
no randomness, no learned weights. They aren't a historical curiosity — they still run inside camera
calibration, visual SLAM front-ends (SLAM = building a map and tracking a camera's position as it
moves, using these classical features), image stabilisation, panorama stitching, and as cheap
preprocessing for the deep models. Seeing them makes the rest of the curriculum legible: you can
*read every line* of what these do, which is rarely true of a learned model.

## Four classics

### Edge detection & contours

*Edge detection* means finding the outlines where image brightness changes sharply. The **Canny**
edge detector (a classic edge-finding recipe; see [Model families](../../models.qmd)) finds
intensity discontinuities (gradients), then thins and links them into clean edges. A *gradient* is
just how fast brightness changes from pixel to pixel — that change is what an edge detector looks
for. **Contours** chain those edges into closed boundaries you can measure (area, perimeter, shape);
a *contour* is a connected boundary line traced around a shape. For decades this *was* "object
detection".

![Canny edges and extracted contours — pure gradient math, no learning.](results/edges_contours.png){#fig-edges}

### Feature detection & matching (ORB)

**ORB** (a fast classical keypoint finder; see [Model families](../../models.qmd)) finds repeatable
keypoints (corners) and encodes each as a binary descriptor, so the *same* physical point can be
matched across two images. Here a *feature* or *keypoint* is a distinctive point — like a corner —
that can be re-found in another image; a *descriptor* is the short numeric fingerprint of that
keypoint used to match it across images; and *feature matching* is pairing the same physical point
between two images. Matching keypoints between views is the engine behind panorama stitching,
structure-from-motion, and the front-end of visual SLAM — the hand-built ancestor of the learned
embeddings in [module 07](../07-retrieval/report.qmd). (A related classical detector you may see
named is SIFT, an older scale-invariant keypoint finder that ORB was designed to replace more
cheaply.)

![ORB keypoints matched between two video frames.](results/features.png){#fig-orb}

### Optical flow

*Optical flow* is the per-pixel motion between two video frames — its direction and speed.
**Farneback** dense optical flow (a classical algorithm that computes this flow for every pixel; see
[Model families](../../models.qmd)) estimates a motion vector for *every* pixel between two frames —
direction as hue, speed as brightness. It's geometry, not recognition: no idea *what* is moving,
only *how*. Flow feeds stabilisation, motion segmentation, and action recognition.

![Dense optical flow between two frames.](results/optical_flow.png){#fig-flow}

## Results

{{< include results/metrics.md >}}

::: {.callout-note title="What to notice"}
- **Essentially free.** Canny runs in well under a millisecond; even dense optical flow is ~100 ms
  on a CPU with no model to load. Compare that to the VLM's ~3.7 s *per answer* on a 24 GB GPU.
- **Transparent and deterministic.** Every output follows from explicit math — no weights, no
  training set, same result every run. When you need to *certify* behaviour, that matters.
- **They still underpin modern systems.** SLAM, calibration, and stitching lean on features and
  flow; deep models often sit *on top of* a classical geometric backbone, not instead of it.
- **The throughline.** ORB→learned embeddings, Canny/contours→learned segmentation, optical
  flow→learned trackers: each deep module in this repo has a classical ancestor solving the same
  problem with hand-designed instead of learned features.
:::

## Where classical CV fails

- **Semantics** — it finds edges and corners, never "cat" or "pedestrian". No notion of meaning.
- **Robustness** — sensitive to lighting, blur, texture, and parameter tuning (those Canny
  thresholds are hand-set).
- **High-level tasks** — captioning, VQA, open-vocabulary anything are out of reach; that's
  exactly the gap deep learning filled.

## Reproduce

```bash
uv sync --group dev      # opencv-python only; no model downloads
uv run python modules/09-classical/run.py
```
